content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Sick day comfort movies

**Score:** 54
**Body:** Out of work with an everything sickness today and looking for something to watch.

Any genre goes, just give me some of your best comfort movies :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lord of the Rings. You can fall asleep at any point and it'll still be on when you wake up.
  - Just in time for second breakfast, or supper?
2. My Cousin Vinny

A League of Their Own

10 Things I Hate About You

Clueless

Really just any movie you’ve watched so many times before that you don’t have to tune in all the way as you know the entire plot by heart.
3. Ferris Beuller's Day Off
4. Young Frankenstein (1974)
5. The Breakfast Club
6. The Fall. Not the show, the film. Can watch it over and over and always find something new ♡
7. The Princess Bride
8. Clue

Cool Runnings

George of the Jungle
9. Goonies

## 2. What are some underrated “life journey” or “soul-searching” movies like The Secret Life of Walter Mitty or The Way (2010)?

**Score:** 38
**Body:** I recently rewatched *The Secret Life of Walter Mitty* and *The Way (2010).* both left me feeling inspired and strangely at peace. They’re not loud blockbusters, but they carry this quiet power about adventure, personal growth, and rediscovering purpose.

What other movies have that same vibe? Something that makes you want to reflect, travel, or just live a little differently after the credits roll.



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Straight Story (1999)  
Into the Wild (2007)
2. The Peanutbutter Falcon.
3. The beach - always makes me want to seek adventure
4. Life Aquatic, Darjeeling Limited. 
5. That one where Reese Witherspoon goes on a hike and Laura Dern is a fox. I can't recall the name right now, sorry, but it was a really lovely film.

Also I agree, The Way is a beautiful film.
  - i guess you are talking about "wild" . i have seen that movie. i googled similar movies to "the way" and that came up as result. 

the way inspired me so much. to step out of comfort zone and experience the life on first hand.
6. Love and monsters.. it has an interesting main point..
7. Under the Tuscan Sun (2003)
8. Perfect days.
9. Wild

And the book by Cheryl Strayed is even better

## 3. What are some underrated 80s comedies that were overlooked because of the classics of the time?

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I'm looking for some suggestions for 80s comedies, specifically ones that aren't well known, or were overlooked in the 80s because something more popular came out. I'm open to just about anything 80s comedy. Reason why is I feel I've watched most of the famous and well known comedies of the 80s. Well, there are lots I still need to watch yet, but I figured I'd get started on some of the lesser known films out there as I'm kind of in the mood to discover some unknowns. 

So any suggestions? Thanks!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Innerspace.


When I was a kid, the big "shrinking" kids film comedy was Honey I Shrunk the Kids. Innerpsace is a little more adult, but it has amazing effects, lots of humor, an off-the-wall performance by Martin Short, and Joe Dante (Gremlins, The Burbs) at the helm. Highly recommend.
  - Innerspace wasn't even appreciated in the 80s, but it's so awesome, and the effects are astounding.
  - 80s Meg Ryan 😍
2. Ruthless People

Better Off Dead

Used Cars
  - Better Off Dead is one of the most underrated comedies. That movie is perfect. 
3. Johnny Dangerously!
4. Time Bandits

Ice Pirates

They Call Me Bruce
5. Big Man on Campus (1989)

3 O’Clock High (1987)

Loverboy (1989)
6. I wasn’t around in the 80s so idk if it’s overlooked but Fletch is a good one
7. Real Genius

## 4. My ex got engaged

**Score:** 25
**Body:** My ex who I loved very much and had a special connection to got engaged and it hurts. I want a movie. Not sure if I want something deep and as hurt as I am or something to get me out of it. Just recommend what you would watch if you were me

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Forgetting Sarah Marshall (2008)
  - Are those happy tissues or sad tissues?
2. Swingers.
  - You’re so money, baby
    - You’re so money you don’t even know it!
3. Silver Lining Playbooks, thematically it's there and it'll make you feel good.
4. Under the Tuscan Sun
5. 500 days of summer
6. I would want something engrossing and familiar, with no or minimal romance. Whenever I get gutpunch news, I always go for either:

-Lord of the Rings trilogy

-Silence of the Lambs
7. Don’t know if it’s too much but eternal sunshine of the spotless mind handles past love really well. Tropic Thunder for something more lighthearted and funny as hell

## 5. Looking for a movie where a clown is central to the story

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I'm interested in finding films where a clown is a main character or a key part of the plot. I'm not necessarily looking for straight-up horror like *It*, but more so any genre, drama, thriller, even comedy, where a clown's role is meaningful and well-developed.

Any recommendations for movies that use clowns in interesting or unexpected ways? I'm open to older classics or newer indie films. Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shakes The Clown - 1991
  - The Citizen Kane of alcoholic clown movies.
2. Quick Change (1990)
3. Death to Smoochy
Ed Norton, Danny Devito and Robin Williams at his creepy scariest.
4. Killer Clowns from Outer Space!

It’s a cultish film either you love it or hate it.  Cotton candy anyone?
5. Terrifier 1, 2, and 3
6. The Apprentice
7. Patch Adams

Joker

The Greatest Show on Earth
8. La Strada (1954)
9. The Day The Clown Cried, if you can find it

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What’s the biggest career mistake an actor has ever made?

**Score:** 2397
**Body:** I’ll start with Rod Taylor declining the role of James Bond because he thought the part was “beneath him”

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terrence Howard demanding more money from marvel Disney to play war machine
  - This is #2 for me behind Taylor declining Bond

I’m not sure if it was Howard’s agent who planted the seed of demanding more money, or it was purely Howard’s ego. Either way, they both lost out on millions 

Also, Don Cheadle’s introduction in Iron Man 2 is amazing “look it’s me, I’m here, deal with it, let’s move on” 😆😆😆
2. OJ Simpson killing his wife put the kibosh on his acting career
  - At least he can rest in peace now knowing his ex-wife’s killer is dead.
    - I wish Norm MacDonald had lived long enough to deliver that line.
      - "Hey! Those are my lucky stabbin' gloves!!"
3. George Lazenby deciding not to do a second Bond movie. Christopher Reeve doing Superman IV. Probably lots of others.
4. Jennifer Grey’s nose job after Dirty Dancing.
  - Yeah unfortunately she did get bullying before the nose job…about her nose. Then she got slack for getting the nose job. I’m sure it messed with her head 🥺
5. David Caruso's flex to leave NYPD Blues turned out poorly

## 2. Whats the absolute WORST movie you ever sat through?

**Score:** 1080
**Body:** Doesn't have to objectively be horribly made. But you HATED the thing, you took it personally.

Maybe it was something that triggered you or made you cringe halfway and you just could not let it go?

Mine probably the most obvious pick but gotta be it: Cats (2019)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That new war of the worlds joint was sooo bad 🤐
2. # Emilia Pérez
3. *The Master of Disguise* was nowhere turtley enough for me. And if you loved it as a kid, don't rewatch it.
  - Supposedly, he was in that outfit when 9/11 happened….
    - thats actually funny as fuck
4. CATS (2019) is the greatest horror/comedy ever made. Saw it opening weekend. People were loosing their minds with rage and hysteria. Full grown adults were crying. I’ve never laughed so hard in my life. 10/10
  - Some men just want to watch the world burn.
5. Fifty shades, the first one. As soon as I learned it was Twilight fanfiction I already knew it was gonna be crap, but my girlfriend at the time told me something like "Watch it before you make your judgment". Fair enough, I did. Oh boy... I thought I was at least going to get some sexy scenes and a "so bad it's good" dialogue. It was as sexy as filing taxes, and the dialogue and acting were just bad, without the "it's good" part.
  - The real BDSM date movie is Secretary.
6. CATS 2019 is my top spot for most hated movie I've ever seen, but I hate it because it serves as both a complete character assassination for EVERY major character and relationship, AND they slaughtered the music. like the music is bad on a technical level. the only musical positives are:

-Jennifer Hudson existing

-Jason Derulo at least has fun and tries in his song

-tap dancing in skimbleshanks the Railway Cat


like. what they did to the mungojerrie & rumpleteazer number is criminal. it's one of the worst songs I've ever heard and the original is one of the catchiest songs in the fucking musical. 

the characters, they just wrecked them. especially grizzabella. she's not trying to get to the heavyside layer. that's not the point of her fucking story. the point is that she's trying to rejoin the jellicle cats and her suffering and desperation and love are what make her worthy of going to the heavyside layer in the end. 

mistofeelees is completely fucked over. he's not some shy, bumbling idiot, he's one of the most powerful characters in the show. and rum tum tugger was literally completely written off!!! they COMPLETELY erased demeter and ruined bombalurina by turning her into a fucking macavity lackey. jennyanydots and bustopher jones are turned into comedy joke humiliation characters solely because they're played by fat actors, despite those two characters being held in extremely high regard in the original show. 

and god, macavity. what the *fuck* was that. 

and then both of these issues culminate in the song "beautiful ghosts", which is original to the movie and added because For Some Fucking Reason they made VICTORIA the main character instead of Jemima (or Syllabub in some productions), even though Jemima literally is one of two main characters in the show. but beautiful ghosts......ohhhhh the hate I have in my heart for that song knows no depth. no end. it is the abyss and it is inescapable. that song not only is pointless for Victoria but also completely negates grizzabella's story, which is THE WHOLE POINT OF THE SHOW. 

genuinely the worst adaptation they could have made

## 3. The original ending for Weapons (2025) was insane

**Score:** 491
**Body:** Came across an interview on YouTube a couple weeks ago and Zach Cregger talks about the original ending to Weapons and it’s pretty insane. He was going to get Justine and Alex to build their own army of people using the witchcraft stick Aunt Gladys uses in the movie and essentially have a huge gang war between Justine/Alex’s crew and Aunt Gladys’s crew. They never shot it but it was in the early story drafts.

Would you have preferred this ending over the one they went with? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/zach-cregger-talks-about-the-original-ending-for-weapons-2025-and-its-insane

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That ending sounds too 'big', what they went with was perfect

I would also think that budget and other constraints would come into play lol
  - I agree. It’s a fun concept though and the idea of Justine and Alex going around trying to find the meanest looking people to be in their army would have been super entertaining.
2. That doesn't sound like an ending, it sounds like a whole other film
  - Or a long running anime franchise.
3. Nah the current ending is better. There’s just something wrong about sending mind controlled gym junkies to beat up mind controlled kids.
  - Yeah, that’s the major problem with this ending. The children are just supposed to die?
4. Weapons: Infinity War
5. The kids chasing her through the houses was hilarious, huge laughs in the screening I went to, anything bigger would not have been as impressive.
6. Isn't that a screenshot from Wolf Man?
7. Thank God this was just in the early drafts because that ending sounds terrible

## 4. What is your Opinion on Tron: Legacy?

**Score:** 380
**Body:** Again I truly loved it, seeing it go from retro to the amazing CGI was impeccable. The return of Flynn and his son trying to help him escape the Grid! And let's not forget DAFT PUNK MADE THE SCORE!!! 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wilde
  - She looked great in this movie. I mean she usually does but she was one of the highlights of this film for sure.
  - I heard in an interview that she was very attached to the character and wanted to flesh out her character's story for the next film. Which sadly, was axed for unimpressive box office results.
2. I liked it a lot. The visuals were great and I liked the story. 

You're messing with my Zen man!
  - He really just needed a new rug to tie everything together.
3. Soundtrack alone stays on regular rotation in my house.
4. It’s Bio digital Jazz, man.
5. Conceptually deeper than it appears. Banging sound track.
6. 7/10 for the movie.


12/10 for the soundtrack 
7. It's a reboot disguised as a sequel, but i still love it.

## 5. Movies That Should Never Be Remade.

**Score:** 289

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Schindler’s List
2. Back to the Future should not be touched
  - Mark my words, October 21st 2035.
3. Tropic Thunder
  - Not going to lie.  If Tom Cruise makes good on [his threat](https://variety.com/2025/film/news/christopher-mcquarrie-tom-cruise-les-grossman-movie-1236410254/) to make a standalone Les Grossman movie?  I'm in.
4. Princess bride
  - To paraphrase Wesley, "there's shortage of *perfect* movies in this world. It would be a pity to damage this one.”
5. Pulp Fiction
6. The Shawshank Redemption (1994)
7. The Lord of the Rings Trilogy.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What decisions led to the first hour of Mission Impossible: The Final Reckoning being so dull?

**Score:** 60
**Body:** The first hour of Mission Impossible: The Final Reckoning is terrible. But the rest of the film is pretty great, with two absolutely stunning action sequences. What do you think is behind the directorial choices that led to that pointlessly flabby first hour?

 Here’s what that hour contains:

* One or two short, fairly un-amazing action scenes, and this film’s only use of rubber masks.
* Exposition concerning the Entity taking over the world. Nothing wrong with establishing some real stakes, but there’s far too much of it.
* Shoehorning of previous films into this film’s continuity. We all know they were written as stand-alone films with no over-arching story, so why insult everyone’s intelligence by expecting us to pretend otherwise?
* Praising Ethan Hunt as basically the saviour of the world. I guess this is OK because he kind of is, but they’re over-doing it.
* “Flash-forwards” to Luther in hospital. They’re not actually flash-forwards, and they don’t contribute anything to the plot, the world-building or anything else. Why were these scenes even left in?
* Talk about the perils of decompression, two different decompression chambers, and what gas he’ll be breathing. Turns out to be of only minor importance to the story, so no idea why they spent so much time setting it up.
* “You have to take the key to the sub, get the device and plug it into the other device that the baddie’s got”. This is really all the set-up the film needs, and could have been done extremely quickly. Or just start in media res, and catch us up on the fly.

Long movies used to be seen as problematic because the longer the film, the fewer showings you can have per day, and the less money you’ll make. So why didn’t this apply here? With the film being so expensive, you think they’d want to get as many bums on seats as possible.

Judging from instalments 5-6 (possibly 7), McQuarrie clearly knows what makes these films work, and what audiences want. So why does he drop the ball so badly with a full hour of wheel spinning? Why not get straight to the good stuff with the submarine sequence, and have an incredible 2 hour movie?

Movies don’t reach their finished form by accident, so these failings are all the result of directorial decisions. But does anyone have any theories or information as to what lay behind those decisions?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. From interviews with McQ it’s clear that there was some course correcting done after the reception and box office of Dead Reckoning so that people who didn’t see that could go into Final Reckoning and not feel lost.

Also Final Reckoning was originally made to be non-linear with some scenes in that first hour placed later in the film but once they saw it didn’t work in the edit everything was laid out linearly which meant that that first hour was not only lacking action but also contained a metric ton of information.
2. I think they worked backwards from the 3 huge set pieces and assumed they'd be able to stitch together a first act that catches the audience up to part 1. They seemed to overestimate the importance of stressing the stakes of part 1. That's just not really necessary with this franchise and it's a misunderstanding that leads to the audience trying to remember things they really don't need to worry about.

Ultimately I wonder if they just failed to shoot sufficient coverage, because you could remove a subplot and condense like 3 scenes into 1, that first act could easily be 20 minutes shorter without losing anything.
3. McQ famously does very long interviews with Empire Magazine after each MI production. In the latest, he explicitly admits that the first hour is bad and was the hardest thing to edit out of all the MI movies he's directed.


Some of the other comments align with his own, but the thing I remember him saying that no one has mentioned has to do with test screenings. McQ is a huge proponent of testing, and screens his movies a thousand times to try and identify and iron out any moments of friction with the audience. For Final Reckoning, he said the first hour is flabby but each piece is there to support something else in the back half, so none of it could be taken out. Basically the stuff is all there because audience screeners got confused without it. But it was originally supposed to be sprinkled throughout the film in a nonlinear way, and audiences didn't like that. Since he couldn't remove it, he felt forced to shove it all in an ungainly first act and have the audience "eat their vegetables" (his words) before they could sit back and watch the rest of the action after everything was established.


Honestly it sounds like he got the dumbest people ever in these test screenings because the premise is not complicated at all, even compared to other MI films.
  - He didn't just make them eat their vegetables, he gave them 29 plates of relentlessly boiled carrots. All I can say is, I hope those test audiences were happy with the end result.
  - I'm curious about how they select the test audience. A majority audience of this movie would either be fans of the series or someone who was actually familiar with at least Dead Reckoning (Part One). I presume the amount of people watching a MI for the first time might've been very less.
4. I feel the whole last two movies were just boring and it came down to a very personal uninteresting macguffin and villain. The new female protagonist was flat. The decision to try to tie in the entity to past movies was a Hail Mary to try to make it more interesting but it just wasn’t.

I remember watching whatever the second to last movie was with the train scene thinking “Jesus can this just end? How many fucking seats so they need to jump from???”

Same with the final plane scene in the last movie. It just wasn’t super interesting or new and came across as tired.
  - Glad someone else feels this way. All of the recent Mission Impossible movies were just bloated bores to me. The stunts are cool but its hard for me to even lock in for them when I don't care about anything else going on in the movie.
5. I think it was largely due to over correcting on why dead reckoning part 1 (whoo what a mouthful) didn't earn as much money as they wanted, even tho it was chopped off by barbenheimer. 

The big first hour of collected poo was due to them thinking that fans didn't check out the recent movies, so they had to recap through exposition. I think the original star wars trilogy is the best case for adding to each movie, as they just expect you to have seen the previous movies and doesn't waste your time recapping or make the audiences that already have watched everything, feel stupid. 

The whole shift to being the final reckoning, as opposed to the original dead reckoning part 2, is that they just thought people didn't see dead reckoning, so they now made it so its the BIG BOY FINALE of mission impossible, rather than the 2nd part of the finale saga. 

Imo, it was already kind of stupid that they needed to make two movies for the finale saga when they didn't have any original source material like a hunger games or deathly hallows. They literally could've just made one big last hurrah movie.
  - I disagree that it was stupid to make two movies, they were riding high off the success of Fallout and a two parter was an exciting idea at the time. It only seems like a bad idea in highsight because they didn't really pull it off.
6. I have to feel the tepid response to Dead Reckoning and the longer gap in production between Dead Reckoning and Final Reckoning than planned (they were supposed to be filmed back to back) had something to do with some changes. For me the biggest difference between the two movies was a massive tonal shift, with Dead Reckoning going for a lighter and, dare I say it, more "marvel humor" tone vs. the absolute dead seriousness of Final Reckoning.

My running theory is a lot of "micro adjustments" ended up ballooning the story and left us with what we got.

## 2. Belladonna of sadness changed my life

**Score:** 59
**Body:** I always hear people say “this movie changed my life” and I used to think it was kind of cliché… until it happened to me. Belladonna of Sadness starts after the “lived happily ever after” that most movies end with, which I found really smart. Instead of showing a fairytale wedding as the final victory, it begins right after Jeanne marries the man she loves and from there, everything unravels.

This movie has so many trigger warnings that I don’t even know which ones to use. It’s very sexually graphic, which I think is necessary to the purpose of the story.

The story is brutal but deeply compelling. Jeanne, a hardworking and beautiful woman in medieval France, is assaulted by the local lord and his men on her wedding night. From that moment, her life spirals: she’s shunned by her community, abandoned by her husband, and pushed further and further into isolation. What I found so powerful is that the movie doesn’t treat this as a singular act of cruelty, it shows how everyone around her, whether actively or passively, plays a role in her destruction. The real villain isn’t just the baron, it’s the entire patriarchal system that sustains him.

The only other significant female character, the baron’s wife, is just as telling. Instead of empathizing with Jeanne, she turns on her, jealous of Jeanne’s beauty and terrified of her freedom. That jealousy feels like another weapon of patriarchy, pitting women against each other so they never unite against the system that oppresses them both. Her fear of Jeanne isn’t just personal, it’s political, Jeanne represents a kind of womanhood she’s been forced to suppress in herself.

Visually, the film blew me away. It doesn’t move like traditional animation. Instead, it unfolds like a series of living paintings, Sometimes it feels like an illuminated manuscript, other times like a psychedelic fever dream. That shifting style mirrors Jeanne’s own transformation I think, the still moments are her attempts at stability, the eruptions of color and chaos are the violence and passion consuming her.

The turning point is Jeanne’s pact with the devil. I personally think the “devil” is the part of Jeanne that society and religion told her to suppress, but I’m interested to hear your theories. What could have been portrayed as pure corruption instead felt like the only way she could reclaim agency and happiness. The same sexuality society used to shame and destroy her becomes her weapon of resistance. That hit me hard.

It helped me understand feminism in a way I hadn’t before. Not just as “equality between men and women” in the abstract, but as the fight against entire structures that normalize exploitation and then call it natural or moral. Jeanne’s tragedy made me see how reclaiming your body, your voice, your desire after such trauma is itself an act of rebellion.

To me, defiance feels like a spark that carries forward, like resistance that can’t be extinguished. I think feminism is about that refusal to submit, even when the cost is sacrificing yourself as Jeanne did.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is a fantastic film and a beautiful swan song for Mushi Productions. The design is otherworldly and the soundtrack is hypnotic. It isn’t an easy film to watch, but very worthwhile.
2. One of my favourite thematic links in this movie is to the patron saint Joan of Arc, with the implication being that she is closer to a religious entity than everyone else, that her pursuit of sexual liberation is what makes her close to heaven.

Continuing on the theme, Jeanne is burned at the stake as if she was a witch, akin to Joan of Arc. It can make your head spin because her sexual liberty is perceived as sacrilegious, yet her liberty creates waves in those around her and subsequently forms an Eden. 

So much of the movie is exploitative of Catholicism while also insisting that the religion and how it's been co-opted by the patriarchy is the evil lust for power and ultimately less representative of the religion that is at the forefront of the patriarchal structure in the first place. 

I love this movie. It is a feminist masterpiece and I think the themes regarding sexuality and self ownership are represented so beautifully through how it reclaims the religious figures that have been misrepresented by the patriarchy.
3. I saw this movie maybe 10 years ago and I don't think I was ready for it. It was at an indie cinema with my girlfriend I had just recently started dating and I think I was expecting a more traditional adult-ish anime. I didn't think it was bad, but I was just overwhelmed. Not with emotional resonance, but out of this sense that I had peered into something forbidden and baffling that I had no right to view, and no right to understand. Like it was art of great intensity that was not for me, and not at the moment I had wanted, not the intention I had for the evening.

I remember I kept waiting for her to be saved, for the happy ending. The disconcerting feeling just grew and grew until I was like, 'ah fuck I've spend this entire film with my stomach clenched.' Maybe that was it, the intention, but there's this sense that I hadn't really opened myself to the experience that the film was offering for its entire runtime. I'm going to put it back on my list and watch it with an open heart.
4. For any 4K Blu-ray fans, the transfer for this one is super good. Almost too good. You can see tiny flaws in the animation process here and there. There’s also a short doc on the disc that I found informative and interesting.

## 3. movies that don’t explain themselves.. just exist and expect u to feel it

**Score:** 24
**Body:** i’m not talkin about confusing plots or artsy editing..
i mean films that just breathe on their own pace.. with no intention to explain themselves to u
no loud emotions.. no backstory spoon-feeding.. just vibes, shadows, and feelings u gotta meet halfway
under the skin did that to me..
also the assassin.. the hand of god.. some tarkovsky stuff too
these films don’t hold ur hand.. they trust u’ll get it — or not.
and that’s kinda the beauty
which films hit u like that?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Picnic At Hanging Rock* (1979). Has the superficial trappings of a mystery movie but is largely plotless and vibes-driven. Potentially one of the most vibes-heavy things I've ever seen. Makes you think it's going to be about 'why/how did xyz mysterious thing happen' but it's not even really touching on that conceit; it's going for arguably something more cosmic. But if you sit with it even without that higher plane of intention, it's like a pulsating beating heart.
2. Sounds like David Lynch to me, to some extent. Although he does do confusing plots, artsy editing and loud emotions. But he definitely doesn’t spoonfeed you, his films are not meant to be analysed so everything falls perfectly into place like a Nolan movie or something.

Nobody truly knows exactly how Lost Highway, Mulholland Drive or Inland Empire fit together. Parts of them make sense and parts don’t. That’s because Lynch makes a lot of creative decisions on the spot, by intuition. Analysing why a random actor says some non sequitur line is a dead end because Lynch probably picked them off the street and shot the scene 5 minutes later without a script. He addresses using non-actors as actors multiple times in his autobiography Room to Dream, purposefully to give it that uncanny vibe. Same with using random household objects to make props on the spot.

That’s basically the definition of a “vibes movie” to me. Connecting parts based on feeling and not logic.
3. I just watched Angel’s Egg and has this feeling. I started realizing there was a lot of religious themes happening and was completely in over my head. Beautiful animation and so incredibly slow paced. Crazy how much less adhd the world was back then.
4. Recently had my first experience with Robert Altman's *3 Women*, which definitely comes to mind in response to this prompt. Truly minimal hand-holding, deliberately disorienting in multiple ways, just a film that asks you meet it where it is. I liked it a lot, and I strongly suspect it's going to improve with rewatch once I know what I'm in for.
5. Tati's *Playtime* is one that I think works on this plane. 

Some people vibe with it, some don't. But you have to accept all the things that it *is* doing, and give up on expecting it to do the things most other films do if you want to enjoy it.
6. You're going to want to watch some Slow Cinema. You've already mentioned Tarkovsky, so maybe try Tsai Ming Liang, Apichatpong Weerasethakul, Lav Diaz, Nuri Bilge Ceylan and Bela Tarr next!
  - I add Michelangelo Frammartino to the list.
7. Some of Haneke's movies are like this. Time of the wolf and The Seventh Continent particularly.

Then theres found footage like The Outwaters (2022), its very much this. How successful it was depends on what mood i'm in when I recall it. 

Maybe something like Possession (1981) really hits the spot, its very raw and wacky, dreamlike. You're left to draw your own conclusions about what's going on.
8. Check out Nuri Bilge Ceylan movies. My favourite one is The Wild Pearl Tree but people generally prefer Once Upon a Time in Anatolia. He is inspired by Tarkovsky as you can see his movies are slow burning masterpieces that seems to aim to be felt rather than to be understood. And also you would be exploring a new country in cinema.
9. Every David Lynch movie is exactly this. Main ones I would recommend are Mulholland Drive, The Straight Story, Inland Empire, and Lost Highway. 


I would say Terrance Malick probably fits this criteria as well. 


Mad God is fantastic for this. Maybe a bit more abstract than you're looking for but it's fantastic. 

## 4. Somersault (2004) - a highly stylised, beautiful and ahead-of-it's time exploration of female sexuality by a future director of Marvel films

**Score:** 14
**Body:** *Somersault* came out in 2004 in Australia and seems to have launched the careers of Abbie Cornish and Sam Worthington; it swept the local Oscars equivalent but (personally for me at least, when I watched it in the years following as a teenager) didn't have the cultural staying power of other successful Australian films of the period. I rewatched it for the first time in years. 

This is sad because even in the 20+ intervening years I haven't really seen a story told like this. Directed by Cate Shortland (who'd go on to be a Marvel director...) Cornish stars as Heidi, a high school senior aged girl who runs away from home and school after she's caught kissing her mother's trashy boyfriend. When she lands in a nearby skiing town, we watch peeking through closed fingers as young Heidi tries to figure out how she can use her sexuality both as a tool to find companionship, lodgings, work, and food whilst also staying physically safe in a world full of men that want to take advantage of her. 

Despite following Heidi in a completely subjective manner, Shortland makes the character's inner world opaque at times. She's mature enough to realise if she hits up men in a bar at night and sleeps with them, she'll have somewhere to stay for the evening, but not old enough to read their cues that they want her gone in the morning. There are moments where it seems like Heidi is being depicted as some sort of Lolita-figure temptress, but then we are shown how she, like the child she is, collects trinkets and scrapbooks interesting fabrics and designs.  

Eventually sheltered by a charitable older woman who runs a small ski resort, for free, she connects with Worthington's Joe, an older guy equally confused about how his body relates to his mind. This isn't a love story by any means but instead an opportunity to contrast the ways different people approach the role sex has in their lives. 

There are two really key scenes in the movie that I think unlock it thematically -- Heidi finally makes a same-aged female friend when she gets a part-time job at a gas station. However, she loses this friendship when it's revealed her friend's father is one of the many men whom hit on Heidi earlier in the film, as part of her attempt to secure herself a place to stay. Heidi is not only shocked, but seems entirely confused about what she did wrong. It's as if, again, she's a much younger person controlling the body of a woman - she knows how it can be powerful but not how it puts her in danger or how the world interprets how she uses her body. 

In another scene, she's introduced to an autistic child and his mother who is teaching him how to manually interpret facial expressions via a chart. Heidi is enraptured by the chart, the process of learning how to interact with others via coursework, as if she can see a way forward from her constant state of confusion about why the world reacts to her attempts to persist.

All this makes the movie sound either plot-heavy or dreary, but it's not. It's shot in a light, handheld (?) manner with a dreamlike score; the movie, for instance, stops in its tracks to let Heidi admire how a shot glass full of spirits mixes liquids when dropped in a glass beer. Again, Shortland shoots the movie subjectively (there's maybe two scenes that aren't from Heidi's POV) while not quite letting us in her mind.

If you can find it, I recommend it. 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Theirs Is the Glory (1946) and A Bridge Too Far (1977)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** After seeing *A Bridge Too Far* God knows how many times (that's a quote), I only just learned of *Theirs Is the Glory*, an earlier film depicting the Battle of Arnhem. People talk about movies that "you couldn't make today", but recreating a battle just a year after it took place, with many of the original participants and in the original location is something you can only do once. I can't imagine how surreal it would have been for them, and who knows whether that added to their trauma or helped them work through it.

The resulting drama is more focused on the rank-and-file experience than *Bridge*, which really is a story of officers and strategists. The sense of what the enlisted men went through and the fight they put up is keener here, and some of the combat scene get more intense than anything in the later film. It is interesting that some of the most memorable moments from the Arnhem section of *Bridge*, like flaming the pillbox, the ill-fated attempt to retrieve a supply box, and Kate ter Horst's reading to the wounded, are direct remakes of scenes from this film.

While *A Bridge Too Far* gives you a splendid view of the Dutch countryside, and also of the entire multinational Operation Market-Garden, this film exclusively shows the British Airborn action, trading grandeur for a claustrophobic feeling of how trapped those men were (once scene where they spell out that they're facing two panzer divisions with some 200 tanks drives home their dire odds better than I'd ever realized). The day-by-day format, with an embedded war correspondent's dispatches as a framing device, conveys much better just how long they were holding on; *Bridge*, despite mentioning in dialogue how many days have passed, makes you feel like General Urquhart barely outlasted John Frost and XXX Corp gave up too easily after Nijmegen, not that they fought hard for five more days before having to accept the withdrawal.

Of course, these aren't accidental differences. Or even if unconscious, they are significant. The difference is right there in the film titles. *Theirs Is the Glory* was released on the first anniversary of VJ day, when celebrating the valour of the retuning troops and of those left behind was the order of the day. *A Bridge Too Far*, released two years after the fall of Saigon, looks a lot more like a crypto–Vietnam film about incompetent leaders sending the troops on a fool's errand, and not even having the gumption to see it through.

Has anyone else seen these two films and have any thoughts? (*Theirs Is the Glory* is on Tubi, if anyone want to find it.)

Has anyone else thought about the post-Vietnam context for *A Bridge Too Far*? I hadn't thought such about that until now.
